subtitle: Bariatric Surgery vs Medical Therapy — 5-Year Summary, Why/How, MCQ & T/F Module

trialDesign.fullName: STAMPEDE — Bariatric Surgery versus Intensive Medical Therapy for Diabetes (5-Year Outcomes)
trialDesign.design: Randomized, nonblinded, single-center trial with 5-year follow-up (extension of original STAMPEDE)
trialDesign.population: 150 patients with type 2 diabetes and BMI 27–43; mean baseline HbA1c 9.2%
trialDesign.sampleSize: n=150 (50 per group: medical vs gastric bypass vs sleeve gastrectomy)
trialDesign.duration: 5-year follow-up; 134 completers; 89% of survivors completed assessment
trialDesign.intervention: Intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 years (with or without diabetes medications)
trialDesign.overview: At 5 years, primary endpoint achieved in 29% bypass, 23% sleeve, vs 5% medical therapy; durable weight loss and fewer medications with surgery.

headings.background: Long-term RCT data comparing bariatric surgery with medical therapy in type 2 diabetes were limited.
headings.design: 150 patients; insulin-stratified randomization; single-center; nonblinded; HbA1c target ≤6.0%.
headings.population: Mean BMI 37; 66% women; 37% BMI <35; diabetes duration predictor of success.
headings.intervention: Gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy plus intensive medical therapy.
headings.comparator: Intensive medical therapy with medication escalation to HbA1c ≤6.0%.
headings.followUp: 5 years; 89% completion among survivors; one crossover and one fistula reoperation.

safetyHighlights: No major late surgical complications except one fistula reoperation
Mild anemia more common in surgical groups; no retinopathy worsening
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Bariatric surgery plus intensive medical therapy maintains superior glycemic control and weight loss over 5 years, including in patients with BMI 27–34.

inclusionCriteria: Type 2 diabetes; BMI 27–43; uncontrolled hyperglycemia on medical therapy
keyTakeaways: Primary endpoint at 5 y: 29% bypass · 23% sleeve · 5% medical.
Weight -23% / -19% / -5%; diabetes duration <8 years predicts HbA1c ≤6.0%.
Surgery with fewer meds; benefits in BMI 27–34 challenge coverage thresholds.

items.title: STAMPEDE 5-Year — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: STAMPEDE evaluated 5-year efficacy and safety of intensive medical therapy alone versus medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy in obese patients with uncontrolled type 2 diabetes.

Methodology: 150 patients (BMI 27–43) randomized to three groups. Primary endpoint: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 years, with or without diabetes medications.

Primary Outcomes: Primary endpoint achieved in 29% (bypass), 23% (sleeve), vs 5% (medical therapy).

Secondary Outcomes: Weight change -23% bypass, -19% sleeve, -5% medical; superior triglycerides, HDL, insulin reduction, and quality of life with surgery.

Safety and Medication Use: Generally safe; one late reoperation for recurrent fistula; surgical patients maintained glycemic control with fewer glucose-, BP-, and lipid-lowering medications.

Conclusions: Bariatric surgery plus intensive medical therapy is significantly more effective than medical therapy alone for resolving hyperglycemia over 5 years in mildly to severely obese type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 y: 29% bypass · 23% sleeve · 5% medical
Weight: -23% / -19% / -5%; diabetes duration <8 y predicts success
Fewer meds with surgery; no retinopathy worsening; BMI 27–34 also benefits
items.reference: Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why was the 5-year follow-up of the STAMPEDE trial conducted?
items.answer: The 5-year follow-up was conducted because long-term results from randomized, controlled trials comparing medical therapy with surgical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes were limited, and data was needed to address questions regarding long-term efficacy, safety, and end-organ disease impact.
keyPoints: The 5-year follow-up was conducted because long-term results from randomized, controlled trials comparing medical therapy with surgical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes were limited, and data was needed to address questions regarding long-term efficacy, safety, and end-organ disease impact.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q1: The 5-year follow-up was conducted because long-term results from randomized, co... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How was the primary endpoint of the trial strictly defined?
items.answer: The primary outcome was defined as achieving a glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level of 6.0% or less at 5 years, which could be achieved either with or without the concurrent use of diabetes medications.
keyPoints: The primary outcome was defined as achieving a glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level of 6.0% or less at 5 years, which could be achieved either with or without the concurrent use of diabetes medications.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q2: The primary outcome was defined as achieving a glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why did the trial utilize stratification based on baseline insulin use during randomization?
items.answer: Stratification according to baseline insulin use was used to ensure that patients with varying severity of diabetes were evenly distributed among the three treatment groups, preventing disease severity from skewing the results.
keyPoints: Stratification according to baseline insulin use was used to ensure that patients with varying severity of diabetes were evenly distributed among the three treatment groups, preventing disease severity from skewing the results.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q3: Stratification according to baseline insulin use was used to ensure that patient... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How did weight loss at 1 year correlate with the long-term primary endpoint?
items.answer: The statistical models showed that the percentage of weight loss a patient achieved at 1 year was significantly associated with their success in achieving the primary endpoint (HbA1c ≤6.0%) at the 5-year mark.
keyPoints: The statistical models showed that the percentage of weight loss a patient achieved at 1 year was significantly associated with their success in achieving the primary endpoint (HbA1c ≤6.0%) at the 5-year mark.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q4: The statistical models showed that the percentage of weight loss a patient achie... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why are the study's findings regarding patients with a BMI of 27 to 34 particularly relevant for health policy?
items.answer: The findings are significant because nearly all financial coverage policies worldwide restrict bariatric surgery to patients with a BMI of 35 or higher. This study showed that mildly obese patients (BMI 27 to 34) also experience durable and superior glycemic improvement after surgery compared to medical therapy.
keyPoints: The findings are significant because nearly all financial coverage policies worldwide restrict bariatric surgery to patients with a BMI of 35 or higher. This study showed that mildly obese patients (BMI 27 to 34) also experience durable and superior glycemic improvement after surgery compared to medical therapy.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q5: The findings are significant because nearly all financial coverage policies worl... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How did bariatric surgery impact the need for cardiovascular medications at 5 years?
items.answer: While overall blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels were similar across all three groups at 5 years, the surgical patients required significantly fewer antihypertensive and lipid-lowering medications to maintain those levels compared to the medical-therapy group.
keyPoints: While overall blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels were similar across all three groups at 5 years, the surgical patients required significantly fewer antihypertensive and lipid-lowering medications to maintain those levels compared to the medical-therapy group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q6: While overall blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels were similar across all ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why do the authors suspect the efficacy gap between medical therapy and surgery may have widened over time?
items.answer: The authors noted a reduction in diabetes medication use in the medical-therapy group after 3 years, suggesting that nonadherence—driven by economic deterrents, side effects, or behavioral maladaptation—may have worsened their long-term outcomes.
keyPoints: The authors noted a reduction in diabetes medication use in the medical-therapy group after 3 years, suggesting that nonadherence—driven by economic deterrents, side effects, or behavioral maladaptation—may have worsened their long-term outcomes.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q7: The authors noted a reduction in diabetes medication use in the medical-therapy ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How did Roux-en-Y gastric bypass compare directly to sleeve gastrectomy regarding weight loss?
items.answer: At 5 years, the gastric-bypass procedure resulted in a significantly greater reduction in body weight (-23%) compared to the sleeve-gastrectomy procedure (-19%).
keyPoints: At 5 years, the gastric-bypass procedure resulted in a significantly greater reduction in body weight (-23%) compared to the sleeve-gastrectomy procedure (-19%).
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q8: At 5 years, the gastric-bypass procedure resulted in a significantly greater red... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why did the researchers identify a diabetes duration of less than 8 years as a critical predictor of success?
items.answer: A duration of diabetes of less than 8 years was the main predictor of achieving an HbA1c level of 6.0% or less, underscoring the physiological importance of early surgical intervention before pancreatic beta-cell function is irreversibly depleted.
keyPoints: A duration of diabetes of less than 8 years was the main predictor of achieving an HbA1c level of 6.0% or less, underscoring the physiological importance of early surgical intervention before pancreatic beta-cell function is irreversibly depleted.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q9: A duration of diabetes of less than 8 years was the main predictor of achieving ... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How did the patients' quality of life change in the medical-therapy group over the 5 years?
items.answer: Despite receiving intensive medical treatment, none of the quality-of-life components improved significantly from baseline for the medical-therapy group, and their scores for bodily pain and emotional well-being actually worsened significantly.
keyPoints: Despite receiving intensive medical treatment, none of the quality-of-life components improved significantly from baseline for the medical-therapy group, and their scores for bodily pain and emotional well-being actually worsened significantly.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y Q10: Despite receiving intensive medical treatment, none of the quality-of-life compo... — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Why do the study results mitigate concerns that rapid glycemic control from surgery might worsen diabetic retinopathy?
items.answer: The results ease these concerns because no significant changes or worsening in retinopathy scores, macular edema, or visual acuity were observed in any of the study groups over the 5-year period.
keyPoints: The results ease these concerns because no significant changes or worsening in retinopathy scores, macular edema, or visual acuity were observed in any of the study groups over the 5-year period.

items.question: How did surgical interventions affect the patients' reliance on insulin therapy?
items.answer: At 5 years, approximately 89% of patients in the surgical groups were successfully maintaining an average HbA1c level of 7.0% without taking any insulin, whereas only 61% of patients in the medical-therapy group were off insulin.
keyPoints: At 5 years, approximately 89% of patients in the surgical groups were successfully maintaining an average HbA1c level of 7.0% without taking any insulin, whereas only 61% of patients in the medical-therapy group were off insulin.

items.question: Why did a patient originally assigned to the medical-therapy group undergo a gastric bypass during year 3?
items.answer: The patient crossed over to receive a gastric bypass due to the clinical failure of their assigned medical treatment, as evidenced by a reported HbA1c level remaining above 9%.
keyPoints: The patient crossed over to receive a gastric bypass due to the clinical failure of their assigned medical treatment, as evidenced by a reported HbA1c level remaining above 9%.

items.question: How did the urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio change across the different treatment groups?
items.answer: At 5 years, the urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio had decreased significantly from baseline in the sleeve-gastrectomy group only, and was significantly lower than the ratio observed in the medical-therapy group.
keyPoints: At 5 years, the urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio had decreased significantly from baseline in the sleeve-gastrectomy group only, and was significantly lower than the ratio observed in the medical-therapy group.

items.question: Why were specific vitamins supplemented for the surgical groups?
items.answer: Because bariatric surgeries alter gastrointestinal anatomy and absorption, patients were instructed to take daily multivitamins, vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and iron to prevent late nutritional complications and anemia.
keyPoints: Because bariatric surgeries alter gastrointestinal anatomy and absorption, patients were instructed to take daily multivitamins, vitamin B12, vitamin D, calcium, and iron to prevent late nutritional complications and anemia.

items.explanation: Mean baseline HbA1c was 9.2% among 134 completers.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 1: Mean baseline HbA1c was 9.2% among 134 completers. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Which group experienced the greatest mean percentage of weight loss from baseline to 5 years?
options: Intensive medical therapy alone
Sleeve gastrectomy
Gastric bypass
Both surgical groups were identical
items.explanation: Gastric bypass had greatest weight loss (-23%).
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 2: Gastric bypass had greatest weight loss (-23%). — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: According to the multivariable logistic model, which of the following was a significant predictor of achieving an HbA1c level ≤6.0%?
options: BMI greater than 40
Duration of diabetes of less than 8 years
Female sex
High baseline use of insulin
items.explanation: Diabetes duration <8 years predicted HbA1c ≤6.0%.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 3: Diabetes duration <8 years predicted HbA1c ≤6.0%. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.explanation: Only 5% of medical-therapy patients achieved primary endpoint.

Visual acuity

items.explanation: Mean baseline BMI was 37.

items.explanation: 0% of surgical patients had ≥5% weight gain.

items.question: Which adverse event was noted to be significantly more common in the two surgical groups than in the medical-therapy group?
options: Fatal myocardial infarction
Bowel obstruction
Stroke
Mild anemia
items.explanation: Mild anemia was more common in surgical groups.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 9: Mild anemia was more common in surgical groups. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: How did Low-Density Lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels differ between the three study groups at 5 years?
options: They were significantly lower in the medical-therapy group.
They were significantly lower in the gastric-bypass group.
They were significantly lower in the sleeve-gastrectomy group.
There were no significant differences among the three groups.
items.explanation: No significant LDL differences among groups at 5 years.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 10: No significant LDL differences among groups at 5 years. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.explanation: Therapy adjusted to target HbA1c ≤6.0%.

items.question: What proportion of the overall patient population included in the 5-year assessment were women?
items.explanation: 66% of patients were women.

options: Physical functioning
Energy-fatigue
General health
Emotional well-being

options: Zero
One
Four
Ten

items.explanation: Triglycerides reduced ~40% in gastric-bypass group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 15: Triglycerides reduced ~40% in gastric-bypass group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

options: 0 patients
2 patients
5 patients
11 patients
items.explanation: Zero medical-therapy patients achieved HbA1c ≤6.0% without medications.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 16: Zero medical-therapy patients achieved HbA1c ≤6.0% without medications. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: Approximately what percentage of the study patients had a BMI of less than 35 at baseline?
items.explanation: ~37% had BMI <35 at baseline.

items.question: Which variable showed a significant reduction in the sleeve-gastrectomy group but not in the gastric-bypass or medical-therapy group at 5 years?
options: Fasting plasma glucose
HDL cholesterol
Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio
Retinopathy score
items.explanation: Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio decreased significantly only in sleeve group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 18: Urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio decreased significantly only in sleeve group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: According to the study, relapse of glycemic control (meeting the primary endpoint at year 1 but not at year 5) was associated with which factor?
options: It was highly associated with significant weight regain.
It was not associated with weight regain.
It was associated with stopping vitamin supplementation.
It was associated with age over 50.
items.explanation: Glycemic relapse was not associated with weight regain.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 19: Glycemic relapse was not associated with weight regain. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.question: At 5 years, approximately what percentage of patients in the intensive medical-therapy group were still taking insulin?
items.explanation: 39% of medical-therapy patients still on insulin (61% off insulin).
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y MCQ 20: 39% of medical-therapy patients still on insulin (61% off insulin). — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: The trial concluded that bariatric surgery was superior to intensive medical therapy for achieving an HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 years.
items.explanation: Surgery superior: 29%/23% vs 5% achieving primary endpoint.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 1: Surgery superior: 29%/23% vs 5% achieving primary endpoint. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: To be eligible for the STAMPEDE trial, a patient was required to have a baseline BMI of at least 35.
items.explanation: Eligibility BMI 27–43, not ≥35 only.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 2: Eligibility BMI 27–43, not ≥35 only. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: At 5 years, the gastric-bypass group experienced significantly greater weight loss than the sleeve-gastrectomy group.
items.explanation: Bypass -23% vs sleeve -19% weight loss.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 3: Bypass -23% vs sleeve -19% weight loss. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Emotional well-being scores worsened significantly in both the medical-therapy alone group and the gastric-bypass group by year 5.
items.explanation: Emotional well-being worsened in medical and bypass groups.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 4: Emotional well-being worsened in medical and bypass groups. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: The 5-year data showed a significant worsening of diabetic retinopathy specifically in patients who underwent bariatric surgery.
items.explanation: No significant retinopathy worsening in any group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 5: No significant retinopathy worsening in any group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Patients assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone reported significant improvements from baseline in their general health quality-of-life scores.
items.explanation: No significant QoL improvement in medical-therapy group.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 6: No significant QoL improvement in medical-therapy group. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Intensive medical therapy alone resulted in an average body weight reduction of 5% at the 5-year mark.
items.explanation: Medical therapy group lost ~5% body weight.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 7: Medical therapy group lost ~5% body weight. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Over 88% of surgical patients successfully maintained an average HbA1c level of 7.0% without the use of insulin at 5 years.
items.explanation: ~89% of surgical patients off insulin with HbA1c ~7.0%.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 8: ~89% of surgical patients off insulin with HbA1c ~7.0%. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Relapse of glycemic control between year 1 and year 5 was shown to be strongly driven by significant weight regain.
items.explanation: Relapse not associated with weight regain.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 9: Relapse not associated with weight regain. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: There were significant differences found in systolic and diastolic blood pressure among the three groups at 5 years.
items.explanation: BP levels similar across groups at 5 years.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 10: BP levels similar across groups at 5 years. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: One patient in the sleeve-gastrectomy group crossed over to the gastric-bypass group during year 4 as a treatment for a recurrent gastric fistula.
items.explanation: One sleeve patient crossed to bypass for recurrent fistula in year 4.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 11: One sleeve patient crossed to bypass for recurrent fistula in year 4. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: The STAMPEDE study was conducted as a randomized, double-blinded, multicenter trial.
items.explanation: Single-center, randomized, nonblinded trial.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 12: Single-center, randomized, nonblinded trial. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Excessive weight gain (≥5% increase over baseline) was reported in 19% of the medical-therapy group but occurred in 0% of patients in the surgical groups.
items.explanation: 19% medical vs 0% surgical had ≥5% weight gain.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 13: 19% medical vs 0% surgical had ≥5% weight gain. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: Patients in the surgical groups required more medications to treat hyperlipidemia and hypertension than the medical-therapy group at 5 years.
items.explanation: Surgical patients required fewer BP and lipid medications.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 14: Surgical patients required fewer BP and lipid medications. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.statement: The reductions in HbA1c levels and BMI observed in the surgical groups were similar between patients with a BMI <35 and those with a BMI ≥35.
items.explanation: Similar HbA1c and BMI reductions in BMI <35 and ≥35 subgroups.
items.reference: STAMPEDE 5Y T/F 15: Similar HbA1c and BMI reductions in BMI <35 and ≥35 subgroups. — Schauer PR, Bhatt DL, Kirwan JP, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy for diabetes — 5-year outcomes. N Engl J Med 2017;376:641-651.

items.stem: Why was the 5-year follow-up of the STAMPEDE trial conducted?
options: A) To evaluate short-term glycemic control outcomes
B) To address limited long-term data on efficacy, safety, and end-organ disease impact in type 2 diabetes
C) To compare surgical therapy costs with medical therapy
D) To assess patient recruitment challenges in RCTs
items.explanation: The STAMPEDE trial's 5-year follow-up aimed to address gaps in long-term efficacy, safety, and end-organ disease impact data from prior RCTs comparing medical and surgical therapies in type 2 diabetes.

items.stem: What was the primary endpoint of the trial strictly defined as?
options: A. Achieving an HbA1c level of 6.0% or less at 5 years, with or without diabetes medications
B. Achieving an HbA1c level of 7.0% or less at 5 years, regardless of medication use
C. Maintaining an HbA1c level of 6.0% or less for 5 years without diabetes medications
D. Achieving an HbA1c level of 6.0% or less at 3 years, with or without diabetes medications
items.explanation: The primary endpoint was specifically defined as an HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 years, with no restriction on medication use.

items.stem: How did weight loss at 1 year correlate with the long-term primary endpoint?
options: A) The percentage of weight loss at 1 year was significantly associated with achieving HbA1c ≤6.0% at 5 years
B) Weight loss at 1 year showed no statistically significant correlation with the 5-year primary endpoint
C) Greater weight loss at 1 year correlated inversely with HbA1c levels at 5 years
D) Weight loss at 1 year correlated strongly with BMI changes rather than HbA1c outcomes
items.explanation: Statistical models demonstrated a significant association between 1-year weight loss and achievement of the 5-year HbA1c target, supporting the role of early weight loss in long-term glycemic control.

items.stem: Why are the study's findings regarding patients with a BMI of 27 to 34 particularly relevant for health policy?
options: A) The findings are significant because nearly all financial coverage policies worldwide restrict bariatric surgery to patients with a BMI of 35 or higher, and the study shows that mildly obese patients (BMI 27 to 34) also experience durable and superior glycemic improvement after surgery compared to medical therapy.
B) The findings are significant because weight loss outcomes after bariatric surgery are consistently greater in patients with a BMI of 27 to 34 than in those with higher BMIs.
C) The findings are significant because long-term complications of diabetes are more common in patients with a BMI of 27 to 34 than in those with higher BMIs.
D) The findings are significant because current guidelines recommend bariatric surgery for all patients with a BMI of 27 to 34 regardless of comorbidities.
items.explanation: The study's findings challenge existing financial coverage policies that restrict bariatric surgery to higher BMI thresholds, demonstrating potential benefits for a broader population.

items.stem: At 5 years, how did bariatric surgery affect the need for cardiovascular medications compared to medical therapy in patients with obesity?
options: A) Surgical patients required more antihypertensive medications due to greater weight loss
B) Medication requirements were identical between surgical and medical-therapy groups
C) Surgical patients required fewer antihypertensive and lipid-lowering medications despite similar blood pressure and LDL cholesterol levels
D) Medical-therapy patients achieved better cardiovascular outcomes with fewer medications
items.explanation: Bariatric surgery reduces medication requirements by improving metabolic parameters, allowing similar clinical outcomes with less pharmacologic intervention.

items.stem: Why do the authors suspect the efficacy gap between medical therapy and surgery may have widened over time?

D) Surgical techniques have improved significantly over the past decade.
items.explanation: Reduced medication use in the medical-therapy group due to nonadherence (economic, side effects, or behavioral factors) likely worsened long-term outcomes, explaining the widening efficacy gap.

items.stem: A patient originally assigned to medical therapy underwent gastric bypass during year 3. What was the most likely reason for this crossover?
options: A) HbA1c level remained above 9% despite medical therapy
B) HbA1c level decreased to 7.5% with medical therapy
C) Patient requested bariatric surgery for weight loss
D) Medical therapy caused severe hypoglycemia
items.explanation: Persistent HbA1c >9% indicates inadequate glycemic control with medical therapy, justifying crossover to gastric bypass in clinical trials.

items.stem: At 5 years, which treatment group demonstrated a significant decrease in urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio from baseline, with values significantly lower than the medical-therapy group?
options: A) Sleeve-gastrectomy group only

C) Both sleeve-gastrectomy and medical-therapy groups
D) Neither group
items.explanation: Sleeve-gastrectomy led to greater reduction in albuminuria compared to medical therapy, consistent with metabolic improvements from weight loss.

items.assertion: Specific vitamins are supplemented for the surgical groups to prevent late nutritional complications and anemia.
items.reason: Because bariatric surgeries alter gastrointestinal anatomy and absorption.
items.explanation: Bariatric surgery alters gastrointestinal anatomy and absorption, leading to malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins (e.g., vitamin D), vitamin B12, calcium, and iron. Supplementation is required to prevent deficiencies and related complications.
